paper_title: Chronology and XRF-Scanner Analysis of Marine Sediments

methods.description: This method establishes the chronology of sediment cores using AMS 14C dating and oxygen-isotope tie-points to provide a timeline for the sediment records.

steps.description: The chronologies of Cores SO185-18506, SO185-18579 and SO185-18460 are based on AMS 14C dating of the surface-dwelling foraminifer Globigerinoides ruber supplemented by two oxygen-isotope tie-points over the LGM part of the record.
steps.searchable_summary: obtain AMS 14C dating and oxygen-isotope data for sediment cores

steps.description: Conventional ages were converted to calendar ages following the protocol detailed in ref. 33. We applied consistent marine reservoir age corrections of 400 years.
steps.searchable_summary: convert conventional ages to calendar ages with reservoir correction

steps.description: An interpolated curve was fitted through the AMS 14C data points using a Stineman function.
steps.searchable_summary: fit interpolated curve through AMS 14C data using Stineman function

methods.description: This method involves analyzing the elemental composition of sediment cores using XRF scanners to infer riverine runoff and aeolian dust contributions.

steps.description: Elemental composition was analysed using the second-generation AVAATECH XRF core scanners at MARUM, University of Bremen and at IfG, Kiel University.
steps.searchable_summary: collect elemental composition data using XRF scanners

steps.description: Raw data spectra were processed by the analysis of X-ray spectra with the Iterative Least square software (WIN AXIL) package from Canberra Eurisys.
steps.searchable_summary: process raw X-ray spectra using WIN AXIL software

steps.description: Results are reported in the natural logarithms of elemental ratios, which provide the most easily interpretable signals of relative changes in chemical composition downcore.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate natural logarithms of elemental ratios
